title: US Extends Russian Oil Sanctions Waiver as Global Inventories Hit Dangerous Lows
subhead: Treasury Secretary Bessent grants 30-day extension for Russian oil imports already at sea, while global inventories deplete rapidly and the US seizes another Iranian-linked tanker in the Indian Ocean.

sections.heading: US Extends Russian Oil Sanctions Waiver Amid Iran War Supply Squeeze
sections.body:
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent announced a 30-day extension for countries to import Russian oil already in tankers at sea, marking a continued policy reversal driven by the global supply disruption from the Iran war. The waiver, first granted in March and renewed in April despite Bessent's earlier statements that it would lapse, reflects the deepening energy crisis as Hormuz closure cuts off Gulf supplies.

According to the Associated Press, Bessent said the extension would help poorer nations in need of oil because China would no longer have the same ability to 'stockpile discounted oil' from Russia. However, critics note the temporary lifting of sanctions helps Russia finance its war in Ukraine. The policy reversal underscores how the Middle East conflict is forcing Washington to balance competing priorities: maintaining pressure on Moscow versus preventing a global energy shock.
sections.whyItMatters: The extension reveals the severity of the supply crunch — the US is prioritizing price stability over sanctions discipline, trading leverage against Russia for immediate economic relief. This sets a precedent that sanctions regimes bend under sustained supply disruptions.

sections.heading: Global Oil Inventories at 'Dangerously Low Levels'
sections.body:
Industry executives are warning the Trump administration that global oil inventories have reached 'dangerously low levels' and prices could spike sharply by mid-to-late June. According to a Politico report cited by Crooks and Liars, fuel makers are increasingly drawing down storage tanks to replace products no longer arriving from the Middle East.

One industry executive granted anonymity said: 'We have shared those concerns at the highest levels of government about what's coming in mid-to-late June... You're hitting tank bottom.' The warnings come as data from the US Energy Information Administration shows countries are depleting strategic reserves to offset the Hormuz closure.
sections.whyItMatters: The inventory drawdown creates a countdown to price shock — even if Hormuz reopened tomorrow, it would take weeks to replenish depleted stocks. The market has absorbed the initial disruption but buffer capacity is nearly exhausted.

sections.heading: US Forces Seize Another Iranian-Linked Tanker in Indian Ocean
sections.body:
US forces conducted an interdiction of the stateless oil tanker Davina in the Indian Ocean overnight, the Pentagon announced on June 5. According to Reuters and Anadolu Agency, the operation targeted a sanctioned vessel as part of ongoing enforcement against networks supporting Iran.

The seizure is the latest in a tit-for-tat series of ship interdictions between US and Iranian forces. US Indo-Pacific Command said the operation would continue maritime enforcement efforts against sanctions evasion networks. This follows Iran's seizure of US-sanctioned tankers in the Gulf of Oman and represents an escalation of the maritime dimension of the conflict far from the Strait of Hormuz.
sections.whyItMatters: The Indian Ocean interdiction shows the US is pursuing Iranian oil exports globally, not just at Hormuz. Each seizure tightens the economic pressure on Tehran but also risks retaliation and further supply disruption.

sections.heading: Saudi Energy Minister Meets Novak in St Petersburg, Acknowledges 'Unprecedented Challenges'
sections.body:
Saudi Energy Minister Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman met Russian Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak at the St Petersburg International Economic Forum on June 4, calling for stability in global energy markets as OPEC+ grapples with disruptions from the Iran and Ukraine wars. According to Al Jazeera, the minister stated: 'The world needs every molecule of energy, and every form of stabilisation to this energy, because without energy security, you will lose sustainability.'

Novak acknowledged that Russian oil production has fallen since the start of the year, blaming unplanned maintenance at refineries — the first explicit admission by a Russian official of lower output. Ukraine has intensified attacks on Russian refineries in recent months. Novak also said 'no one really knows what to expect regarding demand at the moment. In other words, uncertainty has increased.'

Reuters reports that Saudi Arabia, Russia, and five other OPEC+ countries would likely agree to a further hike in their output target for July when they meet on Sunday. However, the closure of Hormuz has rendered output agreements 'largely theoretical' for Gulf producers.
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC+ coordination remains intact publicly, but the public acknowledgment of Russian refinery damage and demand uncertainty reveals fractures. The group is trying to project control while admitting fundamental unpredictability.

sections.heading: China's Oil Import Collapse Helping Contain Prices
sections.body:
China has sharply reduced crude oil imports during the Middle East conflict, with imports dropping from approximately 13 million to 7.5 million barrels per day, according to Moneycontrol. Analysts say this demand destruction is one of the biggest reasons oil prices have not surged far beyond $100 per barrel despite the major supply shock.

Beijing is relying on strategic stockpiles while economic slowdown and accelerated electrification continue to reduce China's underlying oil demand. The demand-side contraction is offsetting some of the supply disruption from the Hormuz closure.
sections.whyItMatters: China's demand collapse is functioning as an unplanned shock absorber for global markets — but it cannot last indefinitely. If Chinese demand rebounds while Hormuz remains closed, the price pressure would intensify dramatically.

sections.heading: France Arrests Russian Captain of Shadow Fleet Tanker
sections.body:
French authorities have taken into custody the Russian captain of the seized oil tanker Tagor, a vessel believed to be part of Moscow's shadow fleet. According to France 24, the French navy detained the Tagor on June 1 in international waters with British assistance after its captain repeatedly refused to comply with orders. The ship was flying a false Cameroonian flag and was heading toward Limbe, Cameroon, after departing Murmansk, Russia.

The vessel is linked to petroleum shipping magnate Mohammad Hossein Shamkhani, son of Ali Shamkhani — the former Iranian security official killed on February 28, the first day of the US-Israeli attacks. The captain faces up to one year in prison and a €150,000 fine. The shipowner, currently being identified, may face the same penalties.
sections.whyItMatters: The Shamkhani connection reveals the integration of Russian and Iranian sanctions evasion networks. Western enforcement is now targeting this convergence directly, with naval interdictions expanding from the Gulf to the Atlantic.

sections.heading: Türkiye-NATO Pipeline Expansion and Turkey-Armenia Energy Diplomacy
sections.body:
Turkey is working to connect underground fuel pipelines as part of a $28 billion NATO infrastructure expansion to enhance alliance fuel security, Bloomberg reports. The initiative comes as Turkey seeks to reduce dependence on Iranian gas — its long-term contract with Iran is expiring — and pursue alternative supply routes.

Separately, Armenia and Turkey are using energy talks to advance normalization. According to Oilprice.com citing Eurasianet, Armenian Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan met Turkish Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar on March 10 to discuss electricity interconnection, nuclear energy, and natural gas cooperation. The talks build on last year's Istanbul visit and aim to integrate Armenia into regional power and gas transmission networks, including through the TRIPP corridor linking Azerbaijan to its Nakhchivan exclave through southern Armenia.

Armenia's need to diversify away from Russian and Iranian energy supplies adds urgency. Armenia signed a nuclear cooperation agreement with the United States in February, seeking alternatives to its aging Metsamor plant and Russian fuel supplies.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is positioning itself as both a NATO energy hub and a regional connector. The dual-track diplomacy — NATO fuel security plus South Caucasus energy integration — could reshape Black Sea and Caspian basin energy flows if successful.

bottomLine: The US is walking a tightrope between sanctions enforcement and supply stability — extending Russian oil waivers while seizing Iranian tankers, as global inventories hit critical lows and prices threaten to spike. OPEC+ coordination is publicly solid but privately strained, with Russia admitting refinery damage and Saudi Arabia acknowledging 'unprecedented challenges.' China's demand collapse is buying time, but the market has limited buffer left. The convergence of Russian-Iranian sanctions evasion networks, evidenced by the Shamkhani-linked tanker seizure, shows how energy geopolitics has merged across conflicts.
sources: Reuters, Al Jazeera, Associated Press, France 24, Forbes, Bloomberg, Oilprice.com/Eurasianet, Moneycontrol, Politico, Crooks and Liars, Anadolu Agency, New York Times
